revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/84450013|かんなぎ]]"
revision.text:
'''かんなぎ'''とは、[[Wu (shaman)|巫]]・神なぎ（神和ぎ・神薙ぎ・神凪）とも表記し、その場合（かみなぎ・かむなぎ）とも読む。kkkkk
== 概要 == 巫（かんなぎ、古くは清音でかむなき）は、神の[[Yorishiro|依り代]]、または神の[[Spirit possession|憑依]]、または[[God|神]]との交信をする行為や、その役割を務める人を表す。詳しくは[[Wu (shaman)|巫]]（ふ、かんなぎ）を参照。 [[Minakata Kumagusu|南方熊楠]]は、『巫女（いちこ）に関することども』で、神社に仕える[[Miko|巫女]]を「かんなぎ」、歩き巫女の類を「みこ」とする。
神社によって、あれおとめ（[[Kamo Shrine|賀茂神社]]）、きね、みかむこ、あねこ、こそ、物忌、宮の女（みやのめ。[[Ōmiwa Shrine|大三輪神社]]）、わか（[[Shiogama Jinja|鹽竈神社]]）、たまよりひめ、をさめ（[[Katori Shrine|香取神宮]]）、をそめ（[[Kibitsu Shrine|吉備津神社]]）、いつきこ（松尾神社）、ないし、女[[Bettō|別当]]、湯立て巫女、等と言う。
[[Suwa-taisha|諏訪神社]]の大市、[[Atsuta Shrine|熱田神宮]]の想の市、鹽竈神社のワカ等、歩き巫女の呼称があることから、[[Kunio Yanagita|柳田國男]]は、元来この二種の巫女は同一の者であって、後に分かれた<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>とする。
== 脚注 == <references /> [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Miko]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto]]

'''Kannagi''' is also written as [[Wu (shaman)|巫]], Kannagi (神和ぎ, 神薙ぎ, Kannagi), in which case it is also read as (kaminagi, kamunagi).
== 概要 == 巫（かんなぎ、古くは清音でかむなき）は、神の[[Yorishiro|依り代]]、または神の[[Spirit possession|憑依]]、または[[God|神]]との交信をする行為や、その役割を務める人を表す。詳しくは[[Wu (shaman)|巫]]（ふ、かんなぎ）を参照。 [[Minakata Kumagusu|南方熊楠]]は、『巫女（いちこ）に関することども』で、神社に仕える[[Miko|巫女]]を「かんなぎ」、歩き巫女の類を「みこ」とする。
神社によって、あれおとめ（[[Kamo Shrine|賀茂神社]]）、きね、みかむこ、あねこ、こそ、物忌、宮の女（みやのめ。[[Ōmiwa Shrine|大三輪神社]]）、わか（[[Shiogama Jinja|鹽竈神社]]）、たまよりひめ、をさめ（[[Katori Shrine|香取神宮]]）、をそめ（[[Kibitsu Shrine|吉備津神社]]）、いつきこ（松尾神社）、ないし、女[[Bettō|別当]]、湯立て巫女、等と言う。
[[Suwa-taisha|諏訪神社]]の大市、[[Atsuta Shrine|熱田神宮]]の想の市、鹽竈神社のワカ等、歩き巫女の呼称があることから、[[Kunio Yanagita|柳田國男]]は、元来この二種の巫女は同一の者であって、後に分かれた<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>とする。
== 脚注 == <references /> [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Miko]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto]]

'''Kannagi''' is also written as [[Wu (shaman)|巫]], Kannagi (神和ぎ, 神薙ぎ, Kannagi), in which case it is also read as (kaminagi, kamunagi).
== Overview == A shaman (kannagi, or in olden times, kamunaki in the Chingon) represents the act of communicating with a [[Yorishiro|devotee]] of a deity, or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a deity, or a [[God|God]], or a person who serves in that role. For more information, see [[Wu (shaman)|Shaman]] (Fu, Kan-nagi). [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book "Ichiko ni kansuru koto", refers to [[Miko|Priestess]]s serving shrines as "kannagi", and to walking priestesses as "miko".
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a [[miko]] who serves the shrine, and the word "miko" is used to refer to a walking miko. [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine]], [[Kibitsu Shrine
Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for walking maidens, such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine|Atatsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>とする。
== 脚注 == <references /> [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Miko]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto]]

'''Kannagi''' are [[Shinto]] [[Shamanism|shamans]]. The term is written with the [[Wu (shaman)|巫]], character in common with Miko, but the term is gender neutral and linked to the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shamans]].
== Overview == A shaman (kannagi, or in olden times, kamunaki in the Chingon) represents the act of communicating with a [[Yorishiro|devotee]] of a deity, or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a deity, or a [[God|God]], or a person who serves in that role. For more information, see [[Wu (shaman)|Shaman]] (Fu, Kan-nagi). [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book "Ichiko ni kansuru koto", refers to [[Miko|Priestess]]s serving shrines as "kannagi", and to walking priestesses as "miko".
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a [[miko]] who serves the shrine, and the word "miko" is used to refer to a walking miko. [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine]], [[Kibitsu Shrine
Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for walking maidens, such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine|Atatsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>とする。
== 脚注 == <references /> [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Miko]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto]]

'''Kannagi''' {{Nihongo|2=かんなぎ}} are [[Shinto]] [[Shamanism|shamans]]. The term has a few different writings, one is the [[Wu (shaman)|巫]], character in common with Miko, however the term is gender neutral and linked to the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shamans]].
== Overview == A kannagi represents the act of communicating with a [[Yorishiro|devotee]] of a deity, or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a deity, or a [[God|God]], or a person who serves in that role. For more information, see [[Wu (shaman)|Shaman]] (Fu, Kan-nagi). [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book "Ichiko ni kansuru koto", refers to [[Miko|Priestess]]s serving shrines as "kannagi", and to [[Wandering Miko|walking priestesses]] as "miko".
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a [[miko]] who serves the shrine, and the word "miko" is used to refer to a walking miko. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for walking maidens, such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine|Atatsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>

'''Kannagi''' {{Nihongo|2=かんなぎ}} are [[Shinto]] [[Shamanism|shamans]]. The term has a few different writings, one is the [[Wu (shaman)|巫]], character in common with Miko, however the term is gender neutral and linked to the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shamans]].
== Writings ==
* 巫 * 神なぎ * 神和ぎ * 神薙ぎ * 神凪

([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine]],

([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for walking maidens, such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine|Atatsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>

'''Kannagi''' {{Nihongo|2=かんなぎ}} are [[Shinto]] [[Shamanism|shamans]]. The term has a few different writings, one is the [[Wu (shaman)|巫]], character in common with Miko, however the term is gender neutral and linked to the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shamans]].
== Overview == A kannagi represents the act of communicating with a [[Yorishiro|devotee]] of a deity, or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a deity, or a [[God]], or a person who serves in that role. For more information, see [[Wu (shaman)|Shaman]] (Fu, Kan-nagi). [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book "Ichiko ni kansuru koto", refers to [[Miko|Priestess]]s serving shrines as "kannagi", and to [[Wandering Miko|walking priestesses]] as "miko".
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a [[miko]] who serves the shrine, and the word "miko" is used to refer to a walking miko. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for walking maidens, such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine|Atatsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== References == <references />
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Miko]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto]]

{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|神なぎ|4=神和ぎ・神薙ぎ・神凪}}''' are [[Shinto]] [[Shamanism|shamans]]. The term has a few different writings, one is the [[Wu (shaman)|巫]], character in common with Miko, however the term is gender neutral and linked to the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shamans]].
== Overview == A kannagi represents the act of communicating with a [[Yorishiro|devotee]] of a deity, or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a deity, or a [[God]], or a person who serves in that role. For more information, see [[Wu (shaman)|Shaman]] (Fu, Kan-nagi). [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book "Ichiko ni kansuru koto", refers to [[Miko|Priestess]]s serving shrines as "kannagi", and to [[Wandering Miko|walking priestesses]] as "miko".
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a [[miko]] who serves the shrine, and the word "miko" is used to refer to a walking miko. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for walking maidens, such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine|Atatsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word Kannagi is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神}} and the word Nagi meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Japanese Characters|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese [[God|Gods]]. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]
== References == <references />
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Miko]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto]]

{{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}

'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|神なぎ|4=神和ぎ・神薙ぎ・神凪}}''' are [[Shinto]] [[Shamanism|shamans]]. The term has a few different writings, one is the [[Wu (shaman)|巫]], character in common with Miko, however the term is gender neutral and linked to the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shamans]].
== Overview == A kannagi represents the act of communicating with a [[Yorishiro|devotee]] of a deity, or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a deity, or a [[God]], or a person who serves in that role. For more information, see [[Wu (shaman)|Shaman]] (Fu, Kan-nagi). [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book "Ichiko ni kansuru koto", refers to [[Miko|Priestess]]s serving shrines as "kannagi", and to [[Wandering Miko|walking priestesses]] as "miko".
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a [[miko]] who serves the shrine, and the word "miko" is used to refer to a walking miko. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for walking maidens, such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine|Atatsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>

# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Japanese Characters|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese [[God|Gods]]. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]]
== See Also ==
* [[Kannabi]] a word of similar etymology

{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|神なぎ|4=神和ぎ・神薙ぎ・神凪・巫}}''' are [[Shinto]] [[Shamanism|shamans]]. The term has a few different writings, one is the [[Wu (shaman)|巫]], character in common with [[Miko]], however the term is gender neutral and linked to the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shamans]].
== Overview == A kannagi represents the act of communicating with a [[Yorishiro|devotee]] of a deity, or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a deity, or a [[God]], or a person who serves in that role. For more information, see [[Wu (shaman)|Shaman]] (Fu, Kan-nagi). [[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book "Ichiko ni kansuru koto", refers to [[Miko|Priestess]]s serving shrines as "kannagi", and to [[Wandering Miko|walking priestesses]] as "miko".
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a [[miko]] who serves the shrine, and the word "miko" is used to refer to a walking miko. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for walking maidens, such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine|Atatsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word Kannagi is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|nagi]] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Japanese Characters|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese [[God|Gods]]. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]]
*
== References == <references />
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Miko]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto]]

{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}

From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].
== References == <references />

{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings

In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Chinese characters created outside of China#Japan|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],

{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].
== References == <references />
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Miko]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia18]] [[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia18]] [[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia04]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]]

'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].
== References == <references />
[[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Miko]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]]

[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].
== References == <references />
[[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Miko]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]]

{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].
== References == <references />

[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.

([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
