{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],

{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].
== References == <references /> {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:5]] [[Category:11 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Miko]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Kannagi (Shinto)]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kannagi (Shinto)]] [[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]]

([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>

In addition, it is one of the few {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].
== References == <references />

The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[kanji]] character as used for the [[Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].

[[Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[Wandering Miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[Atsuta Shrine]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>

In addition, it is one of the few {{ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuji|qq=}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

([[Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],

From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuji|qq=}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[:en:Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[Nageia nagi]].

{{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] character as used for the [[:en:Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[:en:Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[:en:Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[:en:Wandering miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[Shiogama Shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], and Waka of [[Shiogama Shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuji|qq=}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[Tree]], mountain, or [[Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[:en:Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[:en:Myth|Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[:en:Nageia nagi|Nageia nagi]].
== References == <references />
{{draft categories|
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Miko]] [[Category:Shinto]]
}}
[[sn:Kannagi (Shinto)]] [[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:かんなぎ]]

In addition, it is one of the few {{ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuji|qq=}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[:en:Tree|Tree]], mountain, or [[:en:Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[:en:Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[:en:Myth|Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[:en:Nageia nagi|Nageia nagi]].

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11264556]] Wikidata name: kannagi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[:en:Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] character as used for the [[:en:Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[:en:Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[:en:Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[:en:Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[:en:Wandering miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], and Waka of [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuji|qq=}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[:en:Tree|Tree]], mountain, or [[:en:Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[:en:Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[:en:Myth|Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[:en:Nageia nagi|Nageia nagi]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[ja:かんなぎ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11264556]] Wikidata name: kannagi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11264556]] Wikidata name: kannagi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[:en:Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] character as used for the [[:en:Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[:en:Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[:en:Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[:en:Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[:en:Wandering miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], and Waka of [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuji|1=non-Chinese-origin CJKV ideograph|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420697}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[:en:Tree|Tree]], mountain, or [[:en:Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[:en:Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[:en:Myth|Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[:en:Nageia nagi|Nageia nagi]].
== References == <references />

}}
[[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]]

From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[:en:Tree|Tree]], mountain, or [[:en:Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[:en:Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[:en:Myth|Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.

<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ja:かんなぎ]] {{wikidata link|Q11264556}} [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Miko]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[ja:かんなぎ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[ja:かんなぎ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[ja:かんなぎ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[ja:かんなぎ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11264556]] Wikidata name: kannagi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11264556]] Wikidata name: kannagi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are [[:en:Shamanism|shamans]] in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] character as used for the [[:en:Chinese culture|Chinese]] [[:en:Wu (shaman)|Wu shaman]].
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''[[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]]'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a [[:en:Spirit possession|possession]] of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
[[:en:Minakata Kumagusu|Kumagusu Minakata]], in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to [[:en:Wandering miko|wandering ''miko'']] as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ([[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]), Waka ([[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama shrine]]), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], and Waka of [[:en:Shiogama shrine|Shiogama shrine]].<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuji|1=non-Chinese-origin CJKV ideograph|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420697}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for [[:en:Tree|Tree]], mountain, or [[:en:Toponym|place name]]. Most of them relate to places of [[:en:Faith|Belief]] in Japanese [[Shinto]], [[:en:Myth|Myth]] and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree [[:en:Nageia nagi|Nageia nagi]].

Most of them relate to places of {{ill|Faith|en|Faith|WD=Q5410500|lt=Belief}} in Japanese [[Shinto]], {{ill|Myth|en|Myth|WD=Q12827256|lt=Myth}} and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines}} and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree {{ill|Nageia nagi|en|Nageia nagi|WD=Q3274110|lt=Nageia nagi}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[ja:かんなぎ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[ja:かんなぎ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[ja:かんなぎ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kannagi (Shinto)]][[ja:かんなぎ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11264556]] Wikidata name: kannagi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11264556]] Wikidata name: kannagi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:02, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shaman}} {{Other uses|Kannagi (disambiguation)}}{{refimprove|date=February 2022}}
'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are {{ill|Shamanism|en|Shamanism|WD=Q42042|lt=shamans}} in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}} character as used for the {{ill|Chinese culture|en|Chinese culture|WD=Q645917|lt=Chinese}} {{ill|Wu (shaman)|en|Wu (shaman)|WD=Q3687892|lt=Wu shaman}}.
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}}'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a {{ill|Spirit possession|en|Spirit possession|WD=Q1782082|lt=possession}} of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
{{ill|Minakata Kumagusu|en|Minakata Kumagusu|WD=Q6862791|lt=Kumagusu Minakata}}, in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to {{ill|Wandering miko|en|Wandering miko|WD=Q11546650|lt=wandering ''miko''}} as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ({{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}}), Waka ({{ill|Shiogama shrine|en|Shiogama shrine|WD=Q133753|lt=Shiogama shrine}}), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], and Waka of {{ill|Shiogama shrine|en|Shiogama shrine|WD=Q133753|lt=Shiogama shrine}}.<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuji|1=non-Chinese-origin CJKV ideograph|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420697}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for {{ill|Tree|en|Tree|WD=Q10884|lt=Tree}}, mountain, or {{ill|Toponym|en|Toponym|WD=Q7884789|lt=place name}}. Most of them relate to places of {{ill|Faith|en|Faith|WD=Q5410500|lt=Belief}} in Japanese [[Shinto]], {{ill|Myth|en|Myth|WD=Q12827256|lt=Myth}} and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines}} and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree {{ill|Nageia nagi|en|Nageia nagi|WD=Q3274110|lt=Nageia nagi}}.

# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuji|1=non-Chinese-origin CJKV ideograph|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420697}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for {{ill|Tree|en|Tree|WD=Q10884|lt=Tree}}, mountain, or {{ill|Toponym|en|Toponym|WD=Q7884789|lt=place name}}. Most of them relate to places of {{ill|Faith|en|Faith|WD=Q5410500|lt=Belief}} in Japanese [[Shinto]], {{ill|Myth|en|Myth|WD=Q12827256|lt=Myth}} and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines}} and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree {{ill|Nageia nagi|en|Nageia nagi|WD=Q3274110|lt=Nageia nagi}}.

'''{{Nihongo|Kannagi|巫 or 神和ぎ or 神薙ぎ or 神凪}}''' are {{ill|Shamanism|en|Shamanism|qid=Q42042|lt=shamans|1=Shamanism}} in [[Shinto]]. Unlike the similar term ''[[miko]]'', the term is gender neutral. The term has a few different writing styles, one being 巫, which is a shared {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}} character as used for the {{ill|Chinese culture|en|Chinese culture|qid=Q645917|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese culture}} {{ill|Wu (shaman)|en|Wu (shaman)|qid=Q3687892|lt=Wu shaman|1=Wu (shaman)}}.
== Overview == A ''kannagi'' represents the act of communicating with a ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|qid=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}'' of a ''[[kami]]'', or a {{ill|Spirit possession|en|Spirit possession|qid=Q1782082|lt=possession|1=Spirit possession}} of a ''kami'', or a person who serves in that role.
{{ill|Minakata Kumagusu|en|Minakata Kumagusu|qid=Q6862791|lt=Kumagusu Minakata|1=Minakata Kumagusu}}, in his book ''Ichiko ni kansuru koto'', refers to ''[[miko]]'' serving shrines as ''kannagi'', and to {{ill|Wandering miko|en|Wandering miko|qid=Q11546650|lt=wandering ''miko''|1=Wandering miko}} as ''miko''.
Depending on the shrine, the word "kannagi" is used to refer to a ''miko'' who serves the shrine, and the word ''miko'' is used to refer to a wandering ''miko''. ({{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}}), Waka ({{ill|Shiogama shrine|en|Shiogama shrine|qid=Q133753|lt=Shiogama shrine|1=Shiogama shrine}}), Tamayorihime, Osame ([[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]]), Osome ([[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]]), Itsukiko ([[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsuo Shrine]]) [[Suwa-taisha]], [[Kibitsu Shrine (Bitchū)|Kibitsu Shrine]],
[[Kunio Yanagita]] says that these two types of maidens were originally the same person, but were later separated, because there are other names for wandering ''miko'', such as Oichi of [[Suwa-taisha|Suwa Shrine]], Sou-no-ichi of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], and Waka of {{ill|Shiogama shrine|en|Shiogama shrine|qid=Q133753|lt=Shiogama shrine|1=Shiogama shrine}}.<ref>柳田國男 『定本 柳田國男集 第９巻』223頁</ref>
== Etymology == The word ''kannagi'' is derived from {{Nihongo|[[kami]]|神|kan}} and the word [[:ja:なぎ|''nagi'']] meaning a calm state, which has many readings
# {{Nihongo|Nagi|和ぎ}} is a peaceful and calm state, and is mainly used for emotions and circumstances. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|凪}} is synonymous with calming, but nowadays it often refers to the state of the sea where there is no wind and there are no calm waves . In addition, it is one of the few {{ill|Kokuji|lt=Kanji originating in Japan|ja|国字|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuji|1=non-Chinese-origin CJKV ideograph|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420697}} and represents the state where the wind has stopped. # {{Nihongo|Nagi|薙ぎ}} refers to a state in which a mountain is collapsing and becoming flat, or a flat field where vegetation is cut, but it also means to drive it sideways (to remove it). From that , it means cleansing as a Shinto ritual . In addition, there are Nagi Shinto rituals all over Japan, and it is said to be an act of calming storms and winds in the inland regions . A rare character is "𡵢 (Nagi)", which is an Ateji character for Nagi because it means collapse.
There are various kanji notations for "Nagi", and it is often used as a homonym for {{ill|Tree|en|Tree|qid=Q10884|lt=Tree|1=Tree}}, mountain, or {{ill|Toponym|en|Toponym|qid=Q7884789|lt=place name|1=Toponym}}. Most of them relate to places of {{ill|Faith|en|Faith|qid=Q5410500|lt=Belief|1=Faith}} in Japanese [[Shinto]], {{ill|Myth|en|Myth|qid=Q12827256|lt=Myth|1=Myth}} and [[Ko-Shintō|Koshinto]], and are used as part of the names of {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} and Japanese deities. It is also used in the name of [[Izanagi]] and the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]. It is used in the name of the tree {{ill|Nageia nagi|en|Nageia nagi|qid=Q3274110|lt=Nageia nagi|1=Nageia nagi}}.
